nativeRange.habitat: Small, shallow, slow-moving streams draining the Brazilian Shield, with sandy or fine-silt substrate and dense marginal and aquatic vegetation; found in first-order tributaries of the upper Paraná River basin in Brazil.

decorPreferences: dense aquatic plants (Java moss, Amazon sword, Vallisneria)
leaf litter and driftwood for shelter and tannins
low to moderate flow from sponge or gentle HOB filter

diet.notes: The Pygmy Aspidoras is a benthic forager — it hunts along the bottom — and accepts high-quality sinking micro-pellets, crushed flake, and wafers formulated for bottom-dwelling fish. Supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods such as bloodworms, micro-worms, daphnia, and brine shrimp nauplii. In the wild, the diet consists of worms, benthic crustaceans, aquatic insect larvae, and organic detritus. This species should not be relied upon to clean up leftovers from other fish; it needs its own targeted feeding.

goodWith: nano fish (celestial pearl danio, Norman’s lampeye)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — with caution around fry
avoidWith: aggressive or predatory fish that can swallow 3 cm prey
strong-current-producing powerheads unsuitable for slow-water species
fish requiring hard alkaline water incompatible with soft-acid parameters

summary: The Pygmy Aspidoras is a tiny armoured catfish from shallow, slow-moving streams in Brazil's upper Paraná basin, reaching around 3 cm as an adult. Although it resembles small Corydoras, it belongs to the separate genus Aspidoras. Peaceful and diurnal, it does best in groups of six or more in well-planted, soft-water aquariums.
careNotes: Aspidoras lakoi is one of the smallest catfishes available in the aquarium hobby, rarely exceeding 3–3.5 cm. Although it looks superficially similar to dwarf Corydoras, it belongs to the distinct genus Aspidoras, established by Ihering in 1907 and distinguished from Corydoras by the presence of a supraoccipital fontanel (a small opening in the skull). Recent revisions to Corydoradinae taxonomy have left this species unchanged — it remains Aspidoras lakoi, the name first published by Miranda Ribeiro in 1949. The species is native to small, shallow streams draining the Brazilian Shield in the upper Paraná system. These headwater streams experience moderate seasonal temperature swings, dropping to around 15°C in the austral winter and reaching around 26°C in summer, which makes A. lakoi somewhat more cool-tolerant than many tropical catfish. A temperature range of 20–26°C suits it well in an aquarium. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water is recommended, targeting pH 6.0–7.2 and gH 2–12. A fine sand substrate at least 3 cm deep is essential; the barbels (the whisker-like sensory organs around the mouth) erode easily on coarse gravel, and shortened barbels are a common sign of declining water quality. The species is best kept in groups of at least six — smaller groups tend to show stress and reduced activity. Dense planting and leaf litter cover are appreciated as shelter. Gentle filtration — such as a sponge filter or baffled hang-on-back filter — is appropriate, as the species is not well suited to strong currents. Like all members of the family Callichthyidae, A. lakoi is highly sensitive to copper-based medications; only copper-free treatments should be used in any tank housing this species. The locking dorsal and pectoral fin spines can deliver a mildly venomous sting; a soft net and care to avoid direct skin contact are advisable when handling. A. lakoi is capable of facultative air-breathing, meaning it may occasionally gulp air at the surface when oxygen levels are low. This species should only be introduced to a biologically mature tank where ammonia and nitrite both read zero.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the typical Corydoradinae pattern. Conditioning a group with varied live and frozen foods over several weeks is the recommended starting point. Spawning is best triggered in a separate 40–60 L tank with a sponge filter and 3–5 cm fine sand; a 30–40% water change with slightly cooler, well-oxygenated water is generally used to simulate seasonal rainfall. Adults scatter small adhesive eggs on plant leaves and fine-leaved vegetation, and the adults should be removed after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 22–24°C. Free-swimming fry accept infusoria and commercial fry powder initially, transitioning to micro-worms and freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii within the first week. Breeding is considered moderately challenging due to the species’ rarity in trade and sensitivity to water quality.

primary.name: FishBase — Aspidoras lakoi
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and morphological reference. Confirmed: max length 4.0 cm SL (male/unsexed); family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes; native range upper Paraná River basin, South America; freshwater benthopelagic habitat; facultative air-breathing; IUCN Least Concern. Temperature and pH not shown on FishBase page — derived from Aqualog (22–25°C, pH 5.0–6.0) and genus-level cross-references.

additional.name: Aqualog.de — Aspidoras lakoi species page
additional.notes: Provided adult size 3.5–4 cm, temperature 22–25°C, pH 5.0–6.0, tank minimum 60–100 cm, behaviour described as sociable, group, diurnal. pH floor of 5.0 noted but raised slightly in care entry to 6.0 as practical hobbyist minimum aligned with other Aspidoras sources.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish Cat-eLog — Aspidoras lakoi
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database; confirmed family, genus, native range (upper Paraná basin, Brazil), and aquarium husbandry parallels with other Aspidoras. Corydoradinae-standard care notes (fine sand, groups, gentle filtration, sponge filter) cross-referenced.

additional.name: SciELO Brazil — Ecology of upper Paraná stream inhabited by Aspidoras fuscoguttatus
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed ecology study on an upper Paraná first-order stream supporting a congener (A. fuscoguttatus). Documents temperature range 15°C (headwater winter) to 27°C (mouth summer), slightly acidic headwater pH, sandy substrate, and facultative air-breathing behaviour under hypoxia. Applied to A. lakoi as habitat analogue given shared drainage system and ecology.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Aspidoras
additional.notes: Confirmed genus-level taxonomy: distinct from Corydoras by presence of supraoccipital fontanel; established by Ihering 1907; endemic to small shallow Brazilian Shield streams; most species have restricted ranges. Cross-referenced species count and distribution overview.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are scattered and adhered to plant leaves following a cool-water change trigger. Adults should be removed after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days, and the fry initially accept infusoria, progressing to micro-worms and freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii within the first week. Breeding is uncommon in the hobby, in part because the species is rarely available in trade.
